title_en: Our Brains in Sync: The Secret of Musical Duets
title_zh: 我們的大腦在同步：音樂二重奏的秘密
tldr_en: Scientists used wireless brainwave sensors to study pianists playing duets. They found that partners' brains synchronize, and this connection is stronger when their natural playing speeds are similar.
tldr_zh: 科學家使用無線腦波感測器研究鋼琴家們的二重奏。他們發現，搭檔的大腦會同步，而且當兩人天生的彈奏速度相近時，這種連結會更強。
article_en:
### How Do We Sync Up?

Have you ever clapped along to a song with a friend and felt perfectly in time? Or played a sport and moved together without even thinking? This amazing ability is called interpersonal synchrony (the way people coordinate their actions in time). Scientists are curious about how our brains make this happen, especially in music.

### Background: Like Two Clocks on a Wall

Think of two pendulum clocks hanging on the same wall. Over time, they often start swinging together. Scientists use this idea of "coupled oscillators" (systems with a natural rhythm that influence each other) to explain human synchrony. They believe each person has their own internal "clock" or natural speed. For a musician, this is their spontaneous rate (the tempo they naturally choose when playing alone). When two musicians play together, their internal clocks must connect, or "couple." This study asks: how does this coupling work in our brains and in our performance?

### What They Did: Brains and Pianos

Researchers invited 20 pairs of pianists to their lab. To study their brains in a natural way, they used wireless electroencephalography (EEG), a method that records brainwaves using a special cap. This "hyperscanning" (recording multiple brains at once) technique let them record both pianists' brains at the same time.

The experiment had two parts. First, each pianist played a simple melody ("Brother John") alone. This measured their personal spontaneous rate. Second, they played the same melody as a duet. One person was the "Leader" who set the speed, and the other was the "Follower." Then, they switched roles. An important detail: a screen blocked them from seeing each other’s hands, so they could only rely on sound.

### What They Found: Linked Brains, Linked Rhythms

The results were fascinating and had two main parts.

First, they looked at behavior. The scientists found a clear link between the pianists' solo speeds and their duet performance. If two partners had very different spontaneous rates, they had more trouble playing perfectly in time together. The small time gap between their notes, called asynchrony (a lack of perfect timing), was larger. Also, the pianist who naturally played faster tended to hit the notes a little bit earlier in the duet. It seems their faster internal clock was "pulling" the rhythm.

Second, they looked at the brain data. The EEG recordings showed that the brainwaves of the duet partners became synchronized while they played together. Specifically, the energy of their brainwaves at the frequency of the music rose and fell together. To be sure this was a real connection, the scientists compared the data to "surrogate pairs" (data from two pianists who didn't actually play together). The real partners' brains were much more synchronized, proving a true brain-to-brain coupling was happening.

### Why It Matters: Understanding Teamwork

This study shows that when we perform together, something more than just listening and reacting is happening. Our brains seem to link up directly, creating a shared network. This idea of coupled oscillators isn't just a theory; we can see it in both behavior and brain activity.

This research helps us understand the fundamental nature of human cooperation. The same brain mechanisms might be at work when people dance, row a boat, or even have a smooth conversation. Furthermore, using wireless EEG to study musicians in a realistic setting opens up exciting new ways to explore the brain during complex, real-world activities.

### Open Questions: What's Next?

This amazing study also leaves us with new questions. The pianists could only hear, not see, each other. Would adding visual cues, like seeing each other's hands, make the brain-to-brain connection even stronger? Does this brain synchrony happen with beginners, or only with experienced musicians? And what about larger groups, like a string quartet or a rock band? Future research can explore how our brains create the beautiful synchrony we see in all kinds of human teamwork.
article_zh:
### 我們如何同步？

你是否曾和朋友一起跟著歌曲打拍子，感覺完全同步？或是在團隊運動中，不用多想就能和隊友動作一致？這種神奇的能力叫做「人際同步」(interpersonal synchrony)，也就是人們在時間上協調彼此動作的方式。科學家們很好奇，我們的大腦是如何辦到的，尤其是在音樂演奏中。

### 背景知識：像牆上的兩座時鐘

想像兩座掛在同一面牆上的鐘擺。久而久之，它們的擺動時常會變得一致。科學家用這種「耦合振盪器」(coupled oscillators) — 也就是帶有自身節律並會互相影響的系統 — 的概念來解釋人類的同步行為。他們認為每個人都有自己內在的「時鐘」或天生速度。對音樂家來說，這就是他們的「自發速率」(spontaneous rate)，即獨自演奏時自然選擇的節奏。當兩位音樂家合奏時，他們內在的時鐘就必須連結，或稱「耦合」。這項研究想問的是：這種耦合在我們的大腦和演奏行為中是如何運作的？

### 他們做了什麼：大腦與鋼琴

研究人員邀請了20對鋼琴家到實驗室。為了能自然地研究他們的大腦，科學家使用了無線腦波圖 (electroencephalography, EEG)，這是一種用特殊帽子記錄腦波的方法。這種「超掃描」(hyperscanning)（同時記錄多個大腦）技術讓他們可以同時記錄兩位鋼琴家的大腦活動。

實驗分為兩部分。首先，每位鋼琴家單獨彈奏一首簡單的旋律（〈兩隻老虎〉的旋律來源〈雅克修士〉）。這一步是為了測量他們個人的「自發速率」。接著，他們以二重奏的方式合奏同一首旋律。其中一人擔任「領導者」，負責設定速度，另一人則是「跟隨者」。然後，他們再交換角色。一個重要的細節是：研究人員用一個遮蔽物擋住了他們的視線，所以他們無法看到對方的手，只能依靠聽覺來合作。

### 他們發現了什麼：相連的大腦，同步的節奏

結果非常有趣，主要有兩大發現。

首先是行為層面。科學家發現，鋼琴家們獨奏時的速度與他們二重奏的表現有明顯關聯。如果兩位搭檔的「自發速率」差異很大，他們在合奏時就比較難完美地對上拍子。他們音符之間的微小時間差，也就是「異步」(asynchrony) 會比較大。此外，天生彈得比較快的鋼琴家，在二重奏時也傾向於早一點點彈下音符。似乎他們較快的內在時鐘正在「拉動」整個節奏。

其次是大腦數據。EEG的紀錄顯示，在二重奏時，搭檔的腦波變得同步了。具體來說，他們腦波中與音樂節奏相同頻率的能量會一起起伏。為了確認這是一種真實的連結，科學家將這些數據與「代理配對」（surrogate pairs，即兩位並未真正合作的鋼琴家的數據）進行比較。結果顯示，真實搭檔的大腦同步程度遠高於代理配對，證明了合奏時確實發生了「腦對腦」的耦合現象。

### 這為什麼重要：理解團隊合作

這項研究告訴我們，當我們一起表演時，發生的事情不僅僅是「聽到然後反應」。我們的大腦似乎會直接連結起來，形成一個共享的網絡。「耦合振盪器」這個概念不只是一個理論，我們可以在行為和大腦活動中都觀察到它。

這項研究幫助我們理解人類合作的基本性質。同樣的大腦機制可能也應用在人們跳舞、划船，甚至進行一場流暢的對話時。此外，使用無線EEG在真實情境下研究音樂家，也為我們探索大腦在複雜的現實世界活動中的運作，開闢了令人興奮的新途徑。

### 待解問題：下一步是什麼？

這項精彩的研究也為我們帶來了新的問題。實驗中的鋼琴家只能聽見，卻看不見彼此。如果加入了視覺線索，例如讓他們看到對方的手，腦對腦的連結會不會更強？這種大腦同步現象是只發生在經驗豐富的音樂家身上，還是初學者也會有？那如果是更大的團體，像是弦樂四重奏或搖滾樂團，情況又會如何呢？未來的研究可以繼續探索，我們的大腦是如何在各種人類團隊合作中，創造出我們所見到的美妙同步。

glossary.definition_en: The coordination of actions in time between two or more people.
glossary.definition_zh: 指兩人或多人間在時間上的動作協調。

glossary.definition_en: A system that has a natural, repeating rhythm or movement.
glossary.definition_zh: 一個具有天生、重複性節律或運動的系統。

glossary.definition_en: A method to record the electrical activity of the brain using sensors on the scalp.
glossary.definition_zh: 一種使用頭皮上的感測器來記錄大腦電波活動的方法。

glossary.definition_en: A lack of perfect timing between two or more events.
glossary.definition_zh: 指兩個或多個事件之間缺乏完美的時間一致性。

glossary.definition_en: A technique to record brain activity from two or more people simultaneously while they interact.
glossary.definition_zh: 一種在兩人或多人在互動時，同時記錄他們大腦活動的技術。

recall.question_en: What was the main purpose of this study?
recall.question_zh: 這項研究的主要目的是什麼？
options_en: A. To find the best piano players in Germany.
B. To understand how musicians' actions and brains synchronize during a duet.
C. To test a new type of wireless EEG machine.
D. To see if "Brother John" is a difficult song to play.
options_zh: A. 尋找德國最厲害的鋼琴家。
B. 了解音樂家在二重奏時，其行為與大腦如何同步。
C. 測試一種新型的無線腦波儀器。
D. 看看〈雅克修士〉這首歌是否很難彈奏。
recall.hint_en: Look at the 'How Do We Sync Up?' and 'Background' sections.
recall.hint_zh: 參考文章「我們如何同步？」和「背景知識」段落。
recall.explanation_en: The study's goal was to investigate interpersonal synchrony, specifically how pianists' brains and behaviors coordinate when they play music together.
recall.explanation_zh: 研究的目標是探討人際同步，特別是鋼琴家在合奏時，他們的大腦和行為如何協調。

recall.question_en: What did the researchers measure *before* asking the pianists to play together?
recall.question_zh: 在請鋼琴家們合奏*之前*，研究人員測量了什麼？
options_en: A. How many years they had played piano.
B. Their favorite type of music.
C. Their natural, comfortable playing speed (spontaneous rate).
D. Their ability to read music quickly.
options_zh: A. 他們彈了幾年鋼琴。
B. 他們最喜歡的音樂類型。
C. 他們天生且感到舒服的彈奏速度（自發速率）。
D. 他們快速視譜的能力。
recall.hint_zh: 查看「他們做了什麼」段落。
recall.explanation_en: The researchers first measured each pianist's 'spontaneous rate' while playing alone. This was crucial for comparing their individual rhythms to their duet performance.
recall.explanation_zh: 研究人員首先測量每位鋼琴家獨奏時的「自發速率」。這一步非常關鍵，以便將他們各自的節奏與二重奏的表現進行比較。

recall.question_en: What was the key difference in brain activity between real duet partners and 'surrogate' pairs?
recall.question_zh: 真實的二重奏搭檔和「代理配對」之間，大腦活動的主要差異是什麼？
options_en: A. Real partners used more of their brains.
B. Real partners had stronger and more correlated brainwave patterns.
C. Surrogate pairs' brains were completely silent.
D. Real partners' brains showed less activity.
options_zh: A. 真實搭檔使用了更多大腦區域。
B. 真實搭檔的腦波模式更強且關聯性更高。
C. 代理配對的大腦完全沒有活動。
D. 真實搭檔的大腦活動較少。
recall.hint_en: Find the part about brain data in the 'What They Found' section.
recall.hint_zh: 在「他們發現了什麼」段落中，找到關於大腦數據的部分。
recall.explanation_en: The study found that real partners' brainwaves were significantly more synchronized (correlated) than the randomly matched surrogate pairs, proving a true 'brain-to-brain coupling' during their interaction.
recall.explanation_zh: 研究發現，真實搭檔的腦波同步（關聯）程度顯著高於隨機配對的代理組，證明了在他們互動過程中，確實存在「腦對腦的耦合」。

transfer.question_en: Two dancers are preparing for a competition. Dancer A naturally moves at a fast, sharp pace, while Dancer B prefers slower, more fluid movements. Based on this study's findings, what challenge might they face?
transfer.question_zh: 兩位舞者正在準備一場比賽。舞者A天生動作節奏快而俐落，舞者B則偏好較慢、流暢的動作。根據這項研究的發現，他們可能會面臨什麼挑戰？
options_en: A. They will probably have no problems synchronizing.
B. They might find it difficult to stay perfectly in time with each other.
C. Dancer B will easily adapt to Dancer A's faster pace.
D. They will be unable to dance together at all.
options_zh: A. 他們同步時可能不會有任何問題。
B. 他們可能會覺得很難跟彼此完美地對上拍子。
C. 舞者B會輕易適應舞者A的較快節奏。
D. 他們將完全無法一起跳舞。
transfer.hint_en: Think about how 'spontaneous rates' affected the pianists.
transfer.hint_zh: 思考「自發速率」是如何影響鋼琴家們的。
transfer.explanation_en: The study suggests that individuals with different 'spontaneous rates' (natural tempos) have greater asynchrony when they try to perform together. The dancers' different natural rhythms would likely make it harder for them to synchronize perfectly.
transfer.explanation_zh: 研究顯示，當「自發速率」（天生節奏）不同的人試圖合作時，會產生較大的異步。這兩位舞者不同的天生節奏，可能會讓他們更難完美同步。

transfer.question_en: A company wants to improve teamwork on a project that requires close collaboration. Based on the concept of 'coupled oscillators,' which strategy might be most effective?
transfer.question_zh: 一間公司想要改善一個需要密切合作的專案的團隊合作。根據「耦合振盪器」的概念，哪種策略可能最有效？
options_en: A. Make everyone work alone in separate rooms.
B. Encourage team members to find a shared rhythm or workflow early on.
C. Assign the person who works fastest as the leader without any discussion.
D. Tell everyone to ignore their colleagues and focus only on their own part.
options_zh: A. 讓每個人在獨立的房間裡單獨工作。
B. 鼓勵團隊成員及早找到一個共享的節奏或工作流程。
C. 不經討論，直接指派工作最快的人當領導者。
D. 告訴每個人忽略同事，只專注於自己的部分。
transfer.hint_en: How did the musicians achieve synchrony?
transfer.hint_zh: 音樂家們是如何達成同步的？
transfer.explanation_en: The study shows that synchronization happens when individuals 'couple' their internal rhythms. Establishing a shared rhythm or process for the team could help their individual 'oscillators' link up, leading to smoother collaboration.
transfer.explanation_zh: 研究顯示，當個體「耦合」他們內在的節奏時，同步就會發生。為團隊建立一個共享的節奏或流程，可以幫助他們個人的「振盪器」連結起來，從而達成更順暢的合作。

critical.question_en: The study blocked the pianists from seeing each other's hands. What is a potential limitation of this design?
critical.question_zh: 這項研究擋住了鋼琴家們的視線，讓他們看不到彼此的手。這個設計有什麼潛在的限制？
options_en: A. It made the experiment too easy for the pianists.
B. The results might not apply to real-life performances where musicians often use visual cues.
C. The screen could have blocked the EEG signals.
D. It proves that sound is the only thing that matters in music.
options_zh: A. 這讓實驗對鋼琴家來說太簡單了。
B. 這個結果可能不適用於真實的表演，因為音樂家常會使用視覺線索。
C. 遮蔽物可能會阻擋EEG訊號。
D. 這證明了在音樂中，聲音是唯一重要的事情。
critical.hint_en: Think about what happens in a real concert.
critical.hint_zh: 想想看一場真實的音樂會中會發生什麼事。
critical.explanation_en: In many real-world duets or ensembles, musicians watch each other for cues (like a nod or a hand movement). By removing this visual information, the study might not fully capture all the factors that contribute to synchrony in a typical performance.
critical.explanation_zh: 在許多真實世界的二重奏或合奏中，音樂家會觀察彼此的線索（例如點頭或手的動作）。藉由移除這些視覺資訊，這項研究可能無法完全捕捉到在一般表演中促成同步的所有因素。

original_abstract: Interpersonal synchrony refers to the temporal coordination of actions between individuals and is a common feature of social behaviors, from team sport to ensemble music performance. Interpersonal synchrony of many rhythmic (periodic) behaviors displays dynamics of coupled biological oscillators. The current study addresses oscillatory dynamics on the levels of brain and behavior between music duet partners performing at spontaneous (uncued) rates. Wireless EEG was measured from N = 20 pairs of pianists as they performed a melody first in Solo performance (at their spontaneous rate of performance), and then in Duet performances at each partner's spontaneous rate. Influences of partners' spontaneous rates on interpersonal synchrony were assessed by correlating differences in partners' spontaneous rates of Solo performance with Duet tone onset asynchronies. Coupling between partners' neural oscillations was assessed by correlating amplitude envelope fluctuations of cortical oscillations at the Duet performance frequency between observed partners and between surrogate (re-paired) partners, who performed the same melody but at different times. Duet synchronization was influenced by partners' spontaneous rates in Solo performance. The size and direction of the difference in partners' spontaneous rates were mirrored in the size and direction of the Duet asynchronies. Moreover, observed Duet partners showed greater inter-brain correlations of oscillatory amplitude fluctuations than did surrogate partners, suggesting that performing in synchrony with a musical partner is reflected in coupled cortical dynamics at the performance frequency. The current study provides evidence that dynamics of oscillator coupling are reflected in both behavioral and neural measures of temporal coordination during musical joint action.
original_citation: Müller V, Sänger J, Lindenberger U (2021). Behavioral and Neural Dynamics of Interpersonal Synchrony Between Performing Musicians: A Wireless EEG Hyperscanning Study. Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 15:717810.
